coaching.coaching: Plan how the fit_comment no-warning behavioral property will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: the fit_comment no-warning behavioral property

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case (e.g. using `caplog` / `assertNoLogs`) verifying that calling `fit_comment` with an over-limit body produces NO log warnings, and add a corresponding AC asserting the no-warning property — verified by that test.
evidence: Plan: 'a PURE fit-to-limit for the comment-DIFF comparison path (`outbound_comments.py:324`), delegating to `adapters/jira/comment_limits.py`'s `truncate_comment_body` with NO warning (the compare path must not emit send-side warnings)'
Testing: 'Failure/edge: an over-limit comment truncates with today's marker semantics' — covers truncation but not the absence-of-warning property
No AC explicitly requires or verifies that `fit_comment` emits zero log-warnings on over-limit input
ABSENCE: no `assertLogs`/`caplog`/`assert not caplog.records` style check is described for the `fit_comment` truncation path
findings.finding: The `fit_comment` no-warning behavioral property — the primary design motivation for introducing a second method alongside `sanitize_comment` — has no described test case or proving command. The plan states 'NO warning (the compare path must not emit send-side warnings)' but neither the Testing section nor the Acceptance Criteria include a case that asserts `fit_comment` is silent when body exceeds the limit.
findings.location: Testing section / Approach section ('NO warning') / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A developer inadvertently copies the `sanitize_comment` implementation (which logs a warning) into `fit_comment`; all existing tests pass because they only check truncation behavior and marker semantics, while the compare path silently emits send-side warnings in production
The warning-suppression property regresses in a future refactor with no automated safety net
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing → Failure/edge: 'an over-limit body passed to `fit_comment` truncates silently (zero log records at WARNING or above — verified with `caplog` assert).' Add a matching AC: '`fit_comment` never emits a log warning on truncation — verified by a `caplog`-based unit test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the remediation section's consumer description: `apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` **directly from `inbound_translate`**, not via `applier.py`'s re-export; update the rationale for keeping the `applier.py` re-export to name its actual consumers (the `applier.<name>` getattr dispatch table cited in `applier.py`'s module docstring).
evidence: apply_inbound_records.py:27-39 shows `from rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate import ... _normalize_adf_body` — a direct import, not via applier
applier.py:122 shows `_normalize_adf_body` IS re-exported from `inbound_translate`, but `apply_inbound_records.py` does not import from `applier`
Plan says: 'applier.py re-exports those three now-deleted names … so BOTH consumers keep working unchanged' — but apply_inbound_records.py is not a consumer of the applier re-export
findings.finding: The plan's remediation section (point 2) states that `apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` via `applier.py`'s re-export (`__all__` lines 322/326/361/367), but the live code shows `apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` DIRECTLY from `rebar_reconciler.inbound_translate` (line 35) — not via the `applier` re-export path. This means `applier.py`'s re-export of `_normalize_adf_body` is not what serves `apply_inbound_records.py`'s three call sites. The plan's consumer-routing description is incorrect; any future removal of `applier.py`'s re-export would not break `apply_inbound_records.py`, but the plan's stated rationale for keeping the re-export (to avoid breaking `apply_inbound_records.py`) is wrong.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, point 2 — '`applier.py` re-exports those three now-deleted names… keeping `_normalize_adf_body`'; also Added AC: '`inbound_translate._normalize_adf_body` STAYS importable… `apply_inbound_records.py`'s three call sites'
scenarios: A reviewer auditing why `applier.py` re-exports `_normalize_adf_body` would find the stated rationale (serving `apply_inbound_records.py`) is false — the real consumer of the applier re-export is only the legacy `applier.<name>` getattr dispatch table referenced in the module docstring
If the plan is used as a guide for a follow-up cleanup that drops the `applier.py` re-export, the plan's safety argument ('BOTH consumers keep working') is incorrect
findings.suggested_fix: In the remediation point 2, replace 'BOTH consumers keep working unchanged' with: '`apply_inbound_records.py` imports `_normalize_adf_body` directly from `inbound_translate` and is unaffected; `applier.py`'s re-export of `_normalize_adf_body` serves the `reconcile.py` getattr dispatch table and must be retained for that reason.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the remediation claim that `applier.py` re-exports `_ADF_KEY_APPLIER`/`_AdfModule_Applier`/`_load_adf_module` at `__all__` lines 322/326/361/367 — a full codebase search shows these symbols do not exist anywhere; either correct the symbol names to match what actually exists, or remove the phantom cleanup step.
evidence: Plan states: 'applier.py re-exports those three now-deleted names, applier.py drops them from its inbound_translate import list and from __all__ (keeping _normalize_adf_body)'
Plan cites: '__all__ lines 322/326/361/367' for these three names
Search for '_ADF_KEY_APPLIER' across all reconciler files returns no matches
Search for '_AdfModule_Applier' across all reconciler files returns no matches
Search for '_load_adf_module' across all reconciler files returns no matches
applier.py __all__ (lines 309-372) read in full: does not contain any of these three names
applier.py imports from inbound_translate (lines 110-127) do not include any of these three names
findings.finding: The plan's remediation section asserts that applier.py re-exports _ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier, and _load_adf_module from inbound_translate at '__all__ lines 322/326/361/367', and instructs that applier.py must 'drop them from its inbound_translate import list and from __all__ (keeping _normalize_adf_body)'. A full search of the entire codebase shows these three symbols (_ADF_KEY_APPLIER, _AdfModule_Applier, _load_adf_module) do not exist anywhere — not in inbound_translate.py, not in applier.py's imports, and not in applier.py's __all__ (lines 309-372). The specific __all__ line numbers cited (322/326/361/367) do not contain these names. This is a scope-exclusion-style false premise: the plan instructs work ('applier.py drops them') predicated on their existence, but they are absent.
findings.location: Plan-review remediation section, item 2 ('inbound_translate's PUBLIC decode surface') and item 3 ('Scope additions: applier.py')
scenarios: A developer following this plan to implement the applier.py cleanup step finds nothing to delete — the named symbols never existed — leading to confusion about whether the plan's scope description is accurate or whether some other cleanup is needed

A reviewer checking AC compliance cannot verify 'applier.py no longer imports/re-exports _ADF_KEY_APPLIER/_AdfModule_Applier/_load_adf_module' because these were never present, making the AC unverifiable against the plan's stated starting state
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the three phantom symbol names from the remediation scope description. The actual state (verified by reading the files) is that `applier.py` already imports and re-exports `_normalize_adf_body` from `inbound_translate` (line 122, in `__all__` at line 361), and none of the three named dead symbols exist. The remediation item for `applier.py` should be updated to reflect: 'applier.py requires no changes to its import list or __all__ for these three symbols, as they were never present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate all existing Protocol implementors (including test doubles/mocks) for `FieldSanitizer` and `InboundMapper`, acknowledge that adding `fit_comment`/`normalize_rich_text` breaks them, and add either an expand-contract sequence (default implementations on the Protocol first) or a documented back-out plan (e.g. 'revert to lazy-load keys via a feature flag or a one-commit revert').
evidence: 'FieldSanitizer ... gains ONE new method, `fit_comment(body: str) -> str`' — Approach §1
'InboundMapper gains `normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str`' — Approach §2
Scope lists only `adapters/jira/backend.py` and `inbound_fields.py` as implementation sites; no mention of test stubs, mocks, or non-Jira adapter skeletons
No rollback or back-out plan appears anywhere in the plan
T4 criterion: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
findings.finding: The plan adds `fit_comment` and `normalize_rich_text` as new methods to the `FieldSanitizer` and `InboundMapper` Protocol interfaces respectively, which is a breaking change for any existing Protocol implementors (e.g. test doubles, stub backends, or future adapters). The plan scopes only the Jira implementation files (`adapters/jira/backend.py`, `inbound_fields.py`) but does not explicitly enumerate other implementors, acknowledge the break, provide an expand-contract sequence (add the method with a default first, then add it to the Protocol), or describe a rollback/back-out plan if the Protocol extension causes undetected failures.
findings.location: Approach section (FieldSanitizer / InboundMapper Protocol extensions); Scope section
scenarios: A test double implementing FieldSanitizer or InboundMapper exists in the test suite and lacks the new method — mypy/type-checking fails silently or test collection errors obscure root cause
A future adapter skeleton (even a stub) implementing these protocols breaks at import time when the new method is absent
If the port-delegated `_normalize_adf_body` route regresses in an edge case post-merge, there is no described path to revert to the direct lazy-load implementation
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Rollback' subsection: 'If post-merge regression is detected, revert Gerrit change 936 as a single atomic commit; the lazy-load keys and their callers are restored exactly. Before merge, run `grep -r "FieldSanitizer\|InboundMapper" tests/` to enumerate all test doubles and confirm each is updated in this change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docstring-level contract note to FieldSanitizer.fit_comment (no warning, pure truncation) and InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text (ADF-dict→text, str→identity) in _backend.py, and capture the sanitize_comment/fit_comment semantic split in AGENTS.md or a short ADR so future backend implementers have a durable reference.
evidence: 'FieldSanitizer … gains ONE new method, fit_comment(body: str) -> str — a PURE fit-to-limit … with NO warning (the compare path must not emit send-side warnings)'
'InboundMapper gains normalize_rich_text(body: Any) -> str'
No mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan adds two semantically distinct new methods to port Protocols (FieldSanitizer.fit_comment — pure fit, no warning; InboundMapper.normalize_rich_text — ADF-or-identity decode) but names no update to AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design doc capturing the interface contract and the rationale for the sanitize_comment/fit_comment semantic split. A future backend implementer has no durable artifact explaining why fit_comment must suppress warnings while sanitize_comment must emit them.
scenarios: A developer implementing a third backend reads the Protocol stub in _backend.py without context and implements fit_comment with a warning log, breaking the comment-diff path's semantic contract.
The distinction between the two FieldSanitizer methods erodes over time because the rationale is only in this plan document, not in the living codebase documentation.
findings.suggested_fix: In _backend.py, add detailed docstrings to the two new Protocol methods spelling out the no-warning invariant and the ADF-or-identity contract. In AGENTS.md (or a new docs/adr/NNN-port-comment-fit.md), add a one-paragraph note: 'FieldSanitizer exposes two comment surfaces: sanitize_comment (send path, emits a warning on truncation) and fit_comment (compare path, pure truncation, no side effects). Implementations MUST NOT log or emit metrics in fit_comment.' This is the minimal doc needed to keep the contract durable across backend implementations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'byte-identically to today' oracle methodology with explicit-expected-value tests: specify concrete inputs (e.g., a comment of exactly limit+N bytes) and assert the exact output string including truncation marker, so tests verify a known-correct contract rather than snapshotting the current implementation's output.
evidence: 'Happy path: an outbound comment body formats and truncates byte-identically to today via the sanitizer route'

The plan names no specific expected byte counts, truncation markers, or canonical ADF-to-text conversion fixtures that would anchor the tests to a known-correct contract rather than the current implementation's output.

The added AC also uses 'byte-identically': 'comment-diff dedup normalizes ADF-dict bodies to text byte-identically — verified by a comment-diff test with an ADF-dict remote body.' This repeats the oracle pattern.
findings.finding: The Testing section's 'byte-identically to today' phrasing specifies a change-detector oracle methodology: run the old lazy-load path, capture output, run the new port-routed path, assert outputs match. If the old path has a latent bug (e.g., an off-by-one in truncation marker placement, or an edge case in ADF decoding), the oracle-comparison test locks it in and the refactoring story cannot distinguish 'equivalent behavior' from 'equivalent bug.' The plan never commits to tests asserting explicit, known expected values (e.g., a comment of exactly N bytes truncates to M bytes with the literal marker string '…[truncated]').
findings.location: Testing section, Happy path bullet: 'byte-identically to today via the sanitizer route'
scenarios: A latent off-by-one in `comment_limits.truncate_comment_body` is preserved and locked in by the oracle comparison, shipping to production after this refactor.
A future change to the truncation marker string passes the oracle test (because both paths update together) but silently breaks downstream consumers expecting the old marker.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, replace 'byte-identically to today' with: 'an outbound comment body of exactly [limit]+100 bytes truncates to [limit] bytes ending with the marker defined in comment_limits (e.g.,

findings.finding: The plan asserts `outbound_comments.py` will resolve both sanitizer and inbound mapper via `select_backend(load_config())`. The module's own docstring explicitly states it uses 'its OWN lazy `_load_adf` / `_load_comment_limits` loaders (the reconciler modules are spec-loaded under test, where `from . import` does not resolve)'. The plan does not address how `select_backend(load_config())` — which requires `rebar.config` and the backend registry — will work when `outbound_comments.py` is spec-loaded as a standalone module in tests, nor does it specify an injection seam (like the `inbound_mapper=None` fallback in `outbound_differ.py:441`) for `outbound_comments`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
